title: Devilled mackerel
subtitle: Grilled mackerel fillets with a hot, sharp mustard glaze
excerpt: Fresh mackerel fillets coated in a sharp, spiced devilled butter with mustard and cayenne, then grilled until the skin blisters. The combination of oily fish and pungent spicing is one of the best in the British repertoire.
sourceNotes: Devilled fish, including mackerel, is documented in British cookery from the Victorian period. Isabella Beeton includes devilled fish preparations in Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. The technique of coating oily fish in a mustard-and-cayenne devilled butter and grilling is characteristic of nineteenth-century British cookery.

content.text: Devilled mackerel is one of the best things you can do with a fresh mackerel. The fish is rich and oily, which means it can take strong flavour without being overwhelmed. The skin blisters under the grill and the devilled butter melts into the flesh. Mustard and cayenne against the fat of the fish is the right combination.

Mackerel must be very fresh, ideally bought on the day. Stale mackerel smells strongly and the texture is soft and unpleasant. A fresh mackerel has bright eyes, red gills, and stiff flesh. If in doubt, ask your fishmonger when it arrived. The fish should cook skin-side up under a very hot grill.
Make the devilled butter and grill

Heat the grill to its highest setting. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} of soft butter with {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard, {{wholegrain-mustard}} of wholegrain mustard, and {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce. Add the cayenne and {{lemon-juice}} of lemon juice. Season with salt.

Line a grill tray with foil. Lay the {{mackerel-fillets}} fillets skin-side up. Spread the butter over the skin. Grill 8 to 10 cm from the heat for 6 to 8 minutes. The skin should blister and char. The fish is done when the flesh flakes at a gentle press. Scatter with parsley and serve at once with bread and a green salad.

items.fix: The grill must be at full heat and the fish should be 8 to 10 cm from the element. Mackerel skin needs direct, intense heat to blister.
items.cause: Grill is not hot enough, or fish is too far from the heat
items.symptom: Skin is pale and not crisping

items.fix: Fresh mackerel is essential. It deteriorates faster than most fish. If in doubt, buy a different fish for this recipe.
items.symptom: Mackerel smells very strong and the flesh is soft

items.fix: Move the fish further from the grill and watch carefully. The butter will colour quickly; the fish needs 6 to 8 minutes at moderate distance.
items.cause: Grill too hot and fish too close to the element
items.symptom: Devilled butter has burned but fish is still raw

items.fix: The devilled butter should taste bold: sharp, hot, and pungent. Taste it before spreading and increase the cayenne and mustard to suit.

content.text: Devilled preparations in British cooking coat meat or fish in mustard, cayenne, and Worcestershire sauce, then grill or fry quickly. They were most common in the Victorian and Edwardian periods. They appeared at breakfast, as savouries at the end of dinner, and as supper dishes. Mackerel suits the treatment well. Its fatty flesh absorbs strong flavour without losing its character. Oily fish and sharp spicing is a natural pairing. The devilled butter is a simple, practical way to do it.
